(?i) match the remainder of the pattern with the following effective flags: gmi
i modifier: insensitive. Case insensitive match (ignores case of [a-zA-Z])
^ asserts position at start of a line

+ Quantifier — Matches between one and unlimited times, as many times as possible, giving back as needed (greedy)
A-Z a single character in the range between A (index 65) and Z (index 90) (case insensitive)
0-9 a single character in the range between 0 (index 48) and 9 (index 57) (case insensitive)
._!#$%&'*+ matches a single character in the list ._!#$%&'*+ (case insensitive)
\- matches the character - literally (case insensitive)
/=?^_`{|}~() matches a single character in the list /=?^_`{|}~() (case insensitive)
@ matches the character @ literally (case insensitive)

A-Z a single character in the range between A (index 65) and Z (index 90) (case insensitive)
0-9 a single character in the range between 0 (index 48) and 9 (index 57) (case insensitive)
1st Capturing Group ([.-][A-Z0-9]+)*
* Quantifier — Matches between zero and unlimited times, as many times as possible, giving back as needed (greedy)
A repeated capturing group will only capture the last iteration. Put a capturing group around the repeated group to capture all iterations or use a non-capturing group instead if you're not interested in the data
Match a single character present in the list below [.-]
.- matches a single character in the list .- (case insensitive)
Match a single character present in the list below [A-Z0-9]+
\. matches the character . literally (case insensitive)
Match a single character present in the list below [A-Z]{2,22}
{2,22} Quantifier — Matches between 2 and 22 times, as many times as possible, giving back as needed (greedy)
A-Z a single character in the range between A (index 65) and Z (index 90) (case insensitive)
$ asserts position at the end of a line
Global pattern flags
g modifier: global. All matches (don't return after first match)
m modifier: multi line. Causes ^ and $ to match the begin/end of each line (not only begin/end of string)

I was trying to describe something like this:
```python
        if limit:
            limit = int(limit)
            if limit < 1:
                raise ValueError("Limit cannot be less than 1")
            params['limit'] = limit
        if exactly_one:
            params['limit'] = 1
```

Your snippet does the same (except that in your case a ValueError won't be thrown for `exactly_one=True, limit=-1`). Both are fine IMO, pick the one that you prefer.

> To change this (i.e. have a limit other than one and a list of results), we would have to explicitly set exactly_one to false in addition to passing a limit, which is a bit cumbersome in my opinion.

It's the current convention to explicitly set `exactly_one` to False for getting a list. I kinda agree that it looks cumbersome to specify both, but violating that convention doesn't seem reasonable to me.

`exactly_one` is implemented by all geocoders. Basically it means what kind of result does the user expect from this geocoding call. It's universal across all geocoder classes. `limit` is just an optional arg of this specific geocoder which allows to tell the backend a *desired* amount of results. But `limit` in no way controls the real number of locations in a result: it can be less, it can be more. It's up to the backend to decide.

So the idea is that `exactly_one` always takes precedence over `limit`.

Thereby I think there's no need to raise an exception if both are set – from my point of view it seems valid (except the `exactly_one=True` and `limit != 1` case, which can be silently fixed to `limit = 1` without being ambiguous to the API user, given that it's `exactly_one` arg which controls the response type rather than `limit`).

Does that seem reasonable to you?

In add() there was a bug: it checks if (s, p, o, **ctxId**, 'remove') is in self.reverseOps and if it is found it removes (s, p, o, **context**, 'add')

Then, it is generally a bad idea to check if an element is in a list before removing it. Just try to remove and deal with the ValueError exception.

```python
if x in l:
    l.remove(x)
else:
    l.add(y)
```

becomes:

```python
try:
    l.remove(x)
except ValueError:
    l.add(y)
```
It saves an uneeded call to `__contains__`

The usual way that the signals are exposed in aiohttp follows the following pattern::

```python
class Foo:
    def __init__(self):
        self._on_some_event = Signal(self)

    @property
    def on_some_event(self):
        return self._on_some_event

f = Foo()
f.on_some_event.send(arg1, arg2)
```

The interface of the signal is fully exposed, its responsibility of the caller to call it using the `send` method and the arguments to trigger all listeners for a specific event.

The previous snippet shows how the signal is not fully exposed and the `send` method is hidden to allow the `Trace` object add the two first params. 

IMHO the snippet implementation is kinda inconsistent if you compare it with the traditional way of exposing signals. 

To avoid that misinterpretation I preferred to create an ad-hoc method called `send` for the `Trace` object.

I think it is very important that we test that we do the right thing on every OS in `targets.yml` where Python 2.6 is installed. Our plan after this release is to stop supporting Python 2.6 entirely so for any OSes ever specified in `targets.yml` where Python 2.6 is used, we should be upgrading them to a newer version of Python. This is the case for all RHEL 6 based distros such as CentOS 6 and some versions of Amazon Linux.

Also, unfortunately I think these tests have been split for a long time. `test_leauto_upgrades.sh` predates `auto_test.py`. The tests that Yomna wrote in #5232 also use the test farm rather than `auto_test.py` because she determined that it was easier for her to deal with all the OS specific differences there rather than in `auto_test.py`. The test farm tests are also currently run on more systems than `auto_test.py`, `auto_test.py` doesn't actually run a real version of Certbot, and it's currently easier to test an upgrade from a real released version of `certbot-auto` in the test farm.

With that said, these are things we can change and we've actually been talking about that lately in #4947 where I'm pushing for us to rethink how we're doing testing `letsencrypt-auto` and all of it's embedded scripts. Since this PR is blocking the release and neither the test farm tests or `auto_test.py` are included in the release, unless there is a functional problem with the tests here, I'd like to leave this as is and move the conversation about rethinking our testing for certbot-auto to #4947.
